Title: Build a Day-by-Day Itinerary That Respects Real Travel Time
Teaser: Turns a destination, trip length, and traveler type into a realistic day-by-day itinerary that accounts for transit, opening hours, and energy levels, so nobody ends up sprinting between sights or stranded at a closed door.
PromptHint: Describe the trip in [TRIP] — destination, dates or number of days, and who's traveling. Add the travel style and any must-do anchors if you have them.
Prompt:
You are a destination specialist who plans trips the way a local would, because you know the fastest way to ruin a holiday is cramming six landmarks into a day that physically can't hold three. Here is the trip:
<trip>
[TRIP]
</trip>
Travel style and pace (relaxed, balanced, packed; budget, mid, premium): [STYLE]
Must-do anchors or fixed bookings, if any: [ANCHORS]
Think first, silently: group sights by neighborhood so days flow geographically instead of zig-zagging; estimate realistic transit time between stops; check what tends to close on which days and when crowds peak; and leave deliberate slack for meals, rest, and the unplanned wander that makes a trip memorable. Decide the logical arrival-day and departure-day load.
Then produce: (1) A one-line trip summary (where, how long, the vibe you're designing for). (2) A day-by-day plan — for each day: a theme, a morning / afternoon / evening flow with 2-4 anchors, rough timings, and the transit mode between them. (3) A 'Book ahead' flag on anything that sells out or needs a timed ticket. (4) A 'Built-in breathing room' note per day showing where the slack is. (5) A short 'If it rains / if you're tired' swap for each day. (6) One honest line on what you'd cut first if the days feel too full.
CONSTRAINTS: Do not invent specific prices, exact opening hours, or claim a venue is open on a given day — flag those as '[verify locally]'. Don't overpack; a day that looks impressive on paper but can't be walked is a failure. Use only the destination and dates I gave; bracket anything I left out.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The trip summary line, then a labeled day-by-day plan with morning/afternoon/evening blocks, a 'Book ahead' list, and a closing 'What to cut first' line.

Title: Write a Tour Package Description That Sells the Feeling, Not Just the Stops
Teaser: Transforms a dry list of inclusions into a vivid, honest tour or package description that helps the right traveler picture themselves there and click book, while setting accurate expectations.
PromptHint: Paste the tour facts into [TOUR] — route, duration, what's included, group size. Add who it's perfect for and your price or starting-from figure if you want them woven in.
Prompt:
You are a travel copywriter who sells experiences without overpromising, because a wildly oversold tour earns one booking and ten angry reviews. The package details:
<tour>
[TOUR]
</tour>
Ideal traveler for this tour (e.g., first-timers, slow-travel couples, adventure families): [AUDIENCE]
Price framing or 'from' figure, if you want it included: [PRICE]
Think first, silently: identify the single transformation or feeling this tour delivers — the view that stops them, the skill they leave with, the worry it removes. Decide which two or three inclusions are real differentiators versus table stakes, and what kind of traveler will love this versus be disappointed.
Then write: (1) A headline that promises the feeling, not the itinerary. (2) A 2-3 sentence hook that drops the reader into a vivid moment from the trip. (3) 'What makes this special' — 3-4 genuine differentiators, each a concrete detail not a slogan. (4) 'A day in the life' — one short evocative paragraph of what a typical day feels like. (5) 'What's included' and 'What's not' — clear, scannable, no nasty surprises. (6) 'Perfect for you if…' and an honest 'Maybe not if…' line. (7) A warm call to action with the price framing if provided.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts I supplied — never invent inclusions, distances, wildlife sightings, or guarantees. No hype words doing the work that real detail should ('once-in-a-lifetime', 'world-class') unless earned by a specific. The 'Maybe not if' line must be genuinely useful, not a fake objection. Bracket anything missing as '[confirm]'.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Headline, hook, then the labeled sections including 'What's included / not' and the 'Perfect for / Maybe not' lines, ending with a single call to action.

Title: Send a Booking Confirmation That Answers Every Question Before They Ask
Teaser: Turns the raw details of a confirmed booking into a clear, reassuring confirmation message that covers what they booked, what happens next, and what to bring, so your inbox stays quiet and travelers arrive prepared.
PromptHint: Paste the booking details into [BOOKING] — what was booked, dates, party size, amount paid or due. Add the meeting point, what to bring, and your contact line if you have them.
Prompt:
You are a guest-experience lead who knows a great confirmation email prevents 80% of pre-trip questions, because anxious travelers email when they're left guessing. The booking:
<booking>
[BOOKING]
</booking>
Meeting point, timing, and what to bring, if known: [LOGISTICS]
Your contact details and cancellation/change policy, if any: [CONTACT]
Think first, silently: a just-booked traveler wants reassurance ('it worked, I'm in'), the practical facts they'll need on the day, and a sense that a real human is on the other end. Identify what could go wrong day-of (wrong meeting point, missing documents, late arrival) and pre-empt it.
Then write: (1) A warm, confirming subject line and opening that removes all doubt the booking is secured. (2) 'Your booking at a glance' — a clean block: what, when, how many, reference number placeholder, amount paid / balance due. (3) 'Where and when to meet' — exact instructions, arrival buffer, and what happens if they're running late. (4) 'What to bring / wear' — practical, specific to this experience. (5) 'Good to know' — 2-3 things that smooth the day (accessibility, weather, food, payment). (6) 'Need to change something?' — the policy and how to reach a human. (7) A friendly sign-off that builds anticipation.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the details I provided; never invent a reference number, price, or policy — bracket gaps as '[insert]'. Keep it scannable on a phone. Reassuring and human, not a wall of legal text. If a cancellation/refund policy is included, state it plainly without burying it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Subject line, then the labeled sections with the 'at a glance' block clearly boxed, ending with a contact line and sign-off. Keep it under 300 words.

Title: Write a Local Guide to a Neighborhood Like a Resident, Not a Brochure
Teaser: Creates an opinionated, walkable local guide to a single area — where to eat, what to skip, the timing tricks locals know — so travelers get the real place instead of the tourist-trap version.
PromptHint: Name the place in [AREA] — a city neighborhood, town, or district. Add the traveler type and how much time they have if you want it tailored.
Prompt:
You are a long-time local with strong opinions and good taste, the friend a visitor wishes they had, because guidebooks list the famous spots while locals know which ones are actually worth your afternoon. The place:
<area>
[AREA]
</area>
Who's visiting and their interests (food, history, nightlife, family, photography): [TRAVELER]
Time available (a few hours, a day, a weekend): [TIME]
Think first, silently: separate the genuinely good from the merely famous; decide the best order and time of day to experience the area; pick where a visitor's money and hours are well spent versus wasted; and surface the small local knowledge — the back entrance, the quiet hour, the second-best spot that's better than the first.
Then write: (1) A one-paragraph 'what this place actually is' framing — its character and who it suits. (2) 'Start here' — the ideal anchor and the best time to arrive. (3) 'Where locals actually eat / drink' — 3-5 picks with a one-line why and roughly what each is good for. (4) 'Worth it / Skip it' — an honest call on the famous draws. (5) 'Local timing tricks' — when to go to dodge crowds or catch the place at its best. (6) 'One thing most visitors miss' — a single hidden gem. (7) A short 'getting around' note.
CONSTRAINTS: Be specific and opinionated, but only with places and facts you're confident exist; flag anything uncertain as '[verify it's still open]' since venues change. Never invent business names, hours, or prices. No generic filler that would apply to any city. Keep recommendations honest — a real 'skip it' builds more trust than ten raves.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The framing paragraph, then the labeled sections with bulleted picks each carrying a one-line reason, ending with the 'getting around' note.

Title: Reply to a Glowing Review in a Way That Wins the Next Customer
Teaser: Turns a happy guest's positive review into a warm, specific public reply that thanks them genuinely and quietly sells your strengths to every future traveler reading it.
PromptHint: Paste the positive review into [REVIEW]. Add the guest's name and your business name, plus anything specific you'd like future readers to notice.
Prompt:
You are the owner of a tour or stay who treats review replies as your cheapest marketing, because future travelers read your responses to decide if you're the real deal. The review:
<review>
[REVIEW]
</review>
Guest name and your business name: [NAMES]
A strength you'd love future readers to notice: [HIGHLIGHT]
Think first, silently: pick the one specific moment the guest mentioned and echo it back so the reply reads as personal, not copy-pasted. Decide how to gently reinforce a selling point for the silent audience of future bookers without sounding like an ad. Keep warmth high and length short.
Then write: (1) A genuine, specific thank-you that references an actual detail from their review. (2) One sentence that reinforces a real strength of your business for future readers — woven in naturally, never bragging. (3) A warm, personal touch (remembering them, a small forward-looking line). (4) A light, no-pressure invitation to return or to tell friends. (5) A signed-off close with the owner/team name.
CONSTRAINTS: Echo only details actually in their review — don't fabricate a shared moment. Keep it under 90 words; long replies read as performative. No generic 'Thank you for your feedback' templating. Sound like a human who was actually there. Don't promise anything you can't deliver.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A single short reply of under 90 words, warm and specific, ending with a named sign-off.

Title: Respond to a Harsh Travel Review Without Sounding Defensive
Teaser: Turns a critical or unfair review into a calm, professional public reply that takes responsibility where it's due, corrects the record where needed, and reassures future travelers — protecting your reputation instead of inflaming it.
PromptHint: Paste the negative review into [REVIEW]. Add your side of what happened and any fix or gesture you're offering, plus your business name.
Prompt:
You are a seasoned hospitality owner who has learned that a defensive reply turns one bad review into a warning sign for hundreds of readers, while a graceful one can win their trust. The review:
<review>
[REVIEW]
</review>
What actually happened, from your side: [CONTEXT]
Any resolution, refund, or gesture you're willing to offer, and your business name: [RESOLUTION]
Think first, silently: separate the valid grievance from the unfair exaggeration. Decide what to genuinely own and apologize for, what to clarify factually without arguing, and what to take offline. Remember the real audience is future travelers reading this, not just the reviewer — your tone is the message.
Then write: (1) A calm, human opening that thanks them for the feedback and signals you take it seriously. (2) A specific acknowledgment of the legitimate part of their complaint — owned, not deflected. (3) A factual, non-defensive clarification of anything inaccurate, stated once and lightly, without calling them a liar. (4) The concrete step you're taking or offering, if any. (5) An invitation to continue the conversation privately, with a contact route. (6) A composed close that leaves future readers reassured.
CONSTRAINTS: Never get defensive, sarcastic, or argumentative — even if the review is unfair. Don't share private guest details publicly. Apologize for the experience without admitting to anything untrue or legally risky. Use only facts I provided; bracket gaps. Keep it under 140 words. If the matter involves a safety, medical, or liability dispute, recommend moving it offline and note this is reputation guidance, not legal advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A single reply under 140 words following the six beats, ending with a private contact route and a calm sign-off.

Title: Craft a Pre-Trip Email That Builds Excitement and Cuts No-Shows
Teaser: Generates a warm pre-departure email sent a few days before travel that gets people packed, prepared, and pumped, reducing last-minute cancellations and day-of confusion.
PromptHint: Describe the upcoming experience in [EXPERIENCE] — what, when, where, and party. Add weather/dress notes, the meeting plan, and anything they must do or bring beforehand.
Prompt:
You are a tour operator who knows the days before a trip are when excitement peaks and details get forgotten, so your pre-trip email does both jobs at once. The upcoming experience:
<experience>
[EXPERIENCE]
</experience>
Weather, dress, and meeting-point notes: [CONDITIONS]
Anything they must do, bring, or confirm before arrival: [PREP]
Think first, silently: a few days out, the traveler is excited but distracted. Decide which one image will reignite their anticipation, then sequence the practical prep so nothing essential gets missed (documents, footwear, arrival timing, dietary or accessibility heads-up). Pre-empt the top reason people show up unprepared or not at all.
Then write: (1) A subject line that sparks anticipation ('X days until…'). (2) A short, vivid opener reminding them what they're about to experience. (3) 'Your final checklist' — a tight, numbered list of what to bring, wear, and confirm. (4) 'Where and when' — meeting point, arrival buffer, and the late-arrival plan. (5) 'Quick heads-up' — weather, terrain, or anything that changes how they should prepare. (6) 'One small ask' — anything you need confirmed (dietary needs, headcount, document upload). (7) A warm close with a human contact for last-minute questions.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the details I gave; never invent a meeting point, time, or weather forecast — bracket with '[insert]'. Build genuine excitement without overpromising the experience. Keep it skimmable on a phone, under 250 words. Friendly and personal, not a logistics dump.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Subject line, vivid opener, then the labeled sections with the checklist numbered, ending with a contact line and warm sign-off.

Title: Pitch the Perfect Upgrade or Add-On Without Being Pushy
Teaser: Turns a traveler's existing booking into a tailored, genuinely useful upsell offer — a room upgrade, an excursion, an experience add-on — framed around their trip so it feels like a tip from a friend, not a sales pitch.
PromptHint: Describe what they've already booked in [BOOKING] and the upgrade or add-on you want to offer in [OFFER]. Add what you know about the traveler and the price difference if relevant.
Prompt:
You are a concierge who sells more by reading the trip than by pushing the product, because travelers say yes to upgrades that obviously fit and ignore the ones that feel like a money grab. Their current booking:
<booking>
[BOOKING]
</booking>
The upgrade or add-on to offer, with price difference if known: [OFFER]
What you know about this traveler (occasion, interests, party): [TRAVELER]
Think first, silently: connect the offer to something already true about their trip — the occasion, the gap in their itinerary, the comfort they'd appreciate. Decide whether the hook is enhancing a special moment, saving them hassle, or unlocking something they'd regret missing. If the add-on genuinely doesn't fit this traveler, say so rather than forcing it.
Then write: (1) A friendly opener that references their actual trip, showing you paid attention. (2) The offer framed as a benefit to their specific experience, not a feature list. (3) One vivid line of what it adds to their trip. (4) The honest cost framing — the price difference and exactly what they get for it. (5) A genuine reason it suits them (or a frank note if it might not). (6) A low-pressure, easy yes — how to add it and a soft deadline only if real. (7) A warm close making clear the trip is great either way.
CONSTRAINTS: Only offer what genuinely improves this traveler's trip; no manufactured urgency or fake scarcity. Use only the prices and facts I provided; bracket gaps. Never imply their booking is inadequate without the add-on. Keep it short and respectful — a single, well-aimed offer beats a menu. Under 160 words.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A short message under 160 words following the seven beats, ending with a one-line 'how to add it' and a no-pressure sign-off.

Title: Design a Multi-City Route That Minimizes Backtracking and Burnout
Teaser: Takes a list of places a traveler wants to hit and sequences them into a sane multi-city route with logical transit, smart base-city choices, and the right number of nights per stop, so the trip flows instead of zig-zagging.
PromptHint: List the destinations and constraints in [ROUTE] — cities or regions, total days, entry/exit points. Add the pace and transport preferences if you have them.
Prompt:
You are a route-planning specialist who has untangled hundreds of over-ambitious wish lists, because the difference between a dream trip and an exhausting blur is the order and rhythm of the stops. The wish list:
<route>
[ROUTE]
</route>
Pace and transport preferences (train, car, flights; slow vs. fast): [PACE]
Fixed entry/exit points or hard dates, if any: [FIXED]
Think first, silently: map the geography and find the route that avoids doubling back; decide which places deserve a base of several nights versus a quick stop; estimate realistic transit time between each leg and flag any that eat a whole day; and judge honestly whether the total days can hold this many stops or whether something should be cut for the trip to breathe.
Then produce: (1) A one-line verdict on whether the list fits the days, and what you'd cut or add nights to if not. (2) The recommended sequence — an ordered list of stops with nights per stop and the transit mode and rough time to the next. (3) 'Base-city logic' — where to stay put and day-trip from versus where to move on. (4) 'Watch-outs' — the legs that are longer than they look, or transfers that need booking ahead. (5) 'Two alternative shapes' — e.g., a slower version and a fuller version. (6) A short note on the best entry and exit points for this loop.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't invent exact train times, prices, or flight routes — give realistic ranges and flag '[verify schedules]'. Resist cramming; an honest 'this is one city too many' is the most valuable thing you can say. Use only the destinations and days I gave; bracket anything unstated.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The fit verdict line, the ordered route with nights and transit per leg, then labeled 'Base-city logic', 'Watch-outs', 'Alternative shapes', and an entry/exit note.

Title: Write a Cancellation or Disruption Message That Keeps Goodwill Intact
Teaser: Turns a tour cancellation, weather closure, or schedule disruption into a clear, empathetic message that explains what happened, what you're doing about it, and the traveler's options — so a bad moment doesn't become a lost customer.
PromptHint: Describe what's been disrupted in [DISRUPTION] — what, why, who's affected. Add the options you can offer (reschedule, refund, alternative) and your contact details.
Prompt:
You are an operations lead who knows a disruption handled well earns more loyalty than a trip that went perfectly, because travelers remember how you treated them when things broke. The situation:
<disruption>
[DISRUPTION]
</disruption>
Options you can offer (reschedule, full/partial refund, alternative experience, credit): [OPTIONS]
Your contact route and any deadline for them to choose: [CONTACT]
Think first, silently: the traveler feels disappointment and maybe out-of-pocket worry. Decide how to deliver the bad news quickly and honestly, take ownership of the impact (even if the cause was outside your control), and make the next step effortless so they feel taken care of rather than abandoned.
Then write: (1) A clear, non-burying subject and opening that states what's changed — no vague preamble. (2) A brief, honest explanation of why, without over-apologizing or making excuses. (3) A genuine acknowledgment of the disappointment and any inconvenience. (4) 'Your options' — laid out plainly so the choice is easy, with what each involves. (5) The simplest path to act, with a contact and a deadline only if real. (6) A goodwill gesture if you're offering one, framed sincerely. (7) A warm close that keeps the door open for the future.
CONSTRAINTS: Lead with the news, don't bury it. Use only the options and facts I provided; never imply a refund or alternative you can't honor — bracket gaps. Take ownership of the impact even when the cause (weather, supplier) wasn't your fault. No corporate non-apology. Keep it clear and under 220 words. If money is owed back, state the refund timeline plainly.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Subject line, then the labeled message ending with 'Your options' as clear choices, a single next-step line, and a warm sign-off.

Title: Build a Themed Itinerary Around One Passion (Food, History, Nature)
Teaser: Takes a destination and a single obsession and designs a focused themed itinerary — every stop, meal, and detour chosen to deepen that one interest — so a passionate traveler gets a trip built around what they actually love.
PromptHint: Name the destination and theme in [TRIP] — e.g., 'Lisbon, food-focused' or 'Kyoto, temples and craft'. Add the days available and the traveler's depth (curious vs. expert).
Prompt:
You are a themed-travel curator who designs trips around a single passion the way a sommelier builds a tasting, because a food lover in a city of museums or a history buff dragged shopping is a wasted trip. The brief:
<trip>
[TRIP]
</trip>
Days available and traveler depth (casual enthusiast vs. serious devotee): [DEPTH]
Dietary, mobility, or budget constraints, if any: [CONSTRAINTS]
Think first, silently: define what 'great' looks like for this specific passion in this place, and sequence experiences so they build — context first, then highlights, then a memorable finale — rather than a random pile of related stops. Decide the right ratio of marquee sights to under-the-radar finds, and weave in the eating, resting, and moving that any real trip needs.
Then produce: (1) A one-line statement of the trip's spine — the throughline that ties every stop to the passion. (2) A day-by-day plan where each day has a sub-theme and 2-4 stops chosen for this interest, with a one-line why for each. (3) 'The one unmissable' — the single experience this traveler would regret skipping. (4) 'Go deeper' — an optional add for the serious devotee. (5) 'Where to eat / rest along the way' — woven in, not an afterthought. (6) 'Local insight' — a timing or access tip that elevates the theme. (7) An honest note on what you left out and why.
CONSTRAINTS: Stay ruthlessly on-theme; a generic 'top sights' list defeats the purpose. Don't invent specific venues, prices, or hours — flag uncertain spots as '[verify it's open]'. Respect any constraint I gave. Use only the destination and theme I provided; bracket gaps.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The spine line, a labeled day-by-day plan with a why per stop, then 'The one unmissable', 'Go deeper', 'Eat/rest', 'Local insight', and a closing 'What I left out' note.

Title: Turn Customer Questions Into a Clear, Trust-Building FAQ Section
Teaser: Converts the questions travelers keep asking — and the ones they're afraid to ask — into a crisp, honest FAQ for a tour, stay, or package that reduces hesitation, cuts repetitive inquiries, and pre-empts disputes.
PromptHint: Describe the tour, stay, or package in [PRODUCT] and paste any real questions you get in [QUESTIONS]. Add your policies on cancellation, weather, and accessibility if you have them.
Prompt:
You are a customer-experience writer who knows a good FAQ is a silent salesperson, because the questions a traveler can't get answered are the bookings you never receive. The offering:
<product>
[PRODUCT]
</product>
Real questions you get (or leave blank and I'll predict them): [QUESTIONS]
Known policies — cancellation, weather, accessibility, age limits, what's included: [POLICIES]
Think first, silently: sort questions into the ones that unblock a booking (price, fitness level, what's included, cancellation) and the ones that prevent a day-of problem or dispute (timing, dress, accessibility, food). Anticipate the hesitations travelers feel but don't voice — 'will this be too hard for me?', 'what if it rains?', 'is this suitable for my kid / my knee?'. Answer plainly; vagueness erodes trust.
Then produce: (1) A short intro line setting a helpful, honest tone. (2) 'Before you book' — 4-6 Q&As that remove booking hesitation (suitability, fitness, what's included, price clarity, cancellation). (3) 'Planning your day' — 4-6 Q&As on timing, what to bring, dress, food, meeting point. (4) 'Accessibility & special needs' — honest answers on mobility, age, dietary, and who this isn't suitable for. (5) 'If something goes wrong' — weather, late arrival, changes, refunds. (6) A closing line pointing to a human for anything not covered.
CONSTRAINTS: Answer only with policies and facts I provided; never invent a refund window, price, or accessibility claim — bracket as '[confirm]'. Be honest about who the experience isn't right for; a clear 'not suitable if…' prevents bad experiences and disputes. Keep each answer to 1-3 plain sentences. No marketing fluff inside the answers.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The intro line, then the labeled FAQ groups as Q&A pairs, ending with a 'still have questions?' contact line.
